name: Niacinamide
lead: The closest thing skincare has to a default. Reduces sebum, calms erythema, supports barrier, modestly fades pigment, plays well with almost every other active. The reason it isn't celebrated is the same reason it works: nothing dramatic, every time.

whatItIs.body: iacinamide is the amide form of vitamin B3 — water-soluble, photo-stable, and in topical use for over four decades. Inside the cell it serves as a precursor to NAD+ and NADP+, the redox cofactors that drive sebum synthesis, ceramide assembly, and a number of inflammation pathways. That dependency is why niacinamide influences so many endpoints from a single molecule: you are tuning a metabolic input that several skin processes are downstream of.
whatItIs.body2: It is not a scrub, a peel, or a "treatment" in the dramatic sense. It is a quiet metabolic adjustment that, used consistently for eight to twelve weeks, produces small, replicated improvements across oil, redness, pigment, and barrier function — without the irritation curve of a retinoid or the pH demands of an acid.

mechanism.b: Reduces fatty-acid synthesis and triglyceride output from sebocytes. Visible as less midday shine within four weeks.
Inhibits melanosome transfer from melanocyte to keratinocyte — without inhibiting melanin synthesis itself. Result: slower fade than HQ, but cleaner.
Upregulates ceramide and free-fatty-acid synthesis; reduces TEWL. The bedrock effect for compromised, over-exfoliated skin.

evidence.note: 5% niacinamide reduced sebum excretion in multiple Asian-population trials at 4 wk.
Slower than HQ but with cleaner side-effect profile. 4–8% range.
Reduces TEWL-driven flushing; pairs with azelaic.
Upregulates ceramide synthesis. Replicated across vehicles.
Modest, slow effect at 5%. Don't oversell it.

pairings.note: Old 'flush myth' debunked. Apply C first, niacinamide second.
Niacinamide buffers retinoid irritation and rebuilds barrier overnight.
The pigment-blocker stack we recommend for melasma.
Apply AHA, give 20–30 min for pH normalisation, then niacinamide.
Direct chelation reduces both. Use on opposite ends of routine.

products.name: Niacinamide 10% + Matmarine
products.note: Best-in-class Indian formulation. Clean vehicle, real concentration.

products.name: Niacinamide 10% + Zinc 1%
products.note: Cheapest entry. Pilling reports common; zinc serves no proven benefit.

products.name: PM Facial Lotion
products.note: Niacinamide 4% in a barrier-rebuilding moisturiser. Quietly excellent.

faq.a: No. The original 1960s study used pure niacin (not niacinamide) at lab-grade purity and aggressive heat. Modern cosmetic formulations show no clinically meaningful loss when layered correctly.
4–5% covers barrier and oil control. 10% is the upper useful limit for pigment work. Beyond 10% the irritation curve overtakes the benefit curve in our experience.

faq.q: Can I use it twice a day?
faq.a: Yes. Niacinamide is one of the few actives where AM + PM use makes sense. We recommend a 4–5% in a moisturiser for AM, and a 10% serum on PM pigment days.
Mildly. The sebum reduction is real; the anti-inflammatory effect is real. But it is not a substitute for a retinoid or BPO. Treat it as the supporting cast.
